content: 4.1.2 Details of Persons Injured During the Demonstrations of Bhadra 23 and 24

Details of a total of 2,522 persons injured during the demonstrations on Bhadra 23 and 24 had been received as of 2082/10/29 (Source: Ministry of Home Affairs). The detailed information of injured persons has been attached in Annex 4.
4.2. Physical Damage

One of the main mandates of this Commission was to ascertain and analyze the details of damage caused during the movement by the Gen Z generation on Bhadra 23 and 24. Accordingly, a public notice was issued specifying a format for requesting damage details from across the country. Interaction programs were conducted under the leadership of the Commission's chairperson and members in the most affected districts, and damage details were also collected. In addition, separate correspondence was made with various agencies to obtain accurate and factual damage details.
According to the details received by this Commission, the total physical damage amounts to approximately NPR 41 billion 89 lakh 18 thousand 709 only.

Regarding the verification of physical damage caused during the demonstrations on Bhadra 23 and 24, a committee was formed under the convenorship of the Secretary of the National Planning Commission as per the Government of Nepal's decision dated 2082/06/04 to prepare a damage assessment and public infrastructure reconstruction plan. That committee conducted on-site inspections, observations, analyses and damage verification in all districts and affected locations, and submitted its report to the Government of Nepal on 2082/08/24. The estimated damage amount therein stands at NPR 84 billion 45 crore 77 lakh.

Due to the nature of the Commission's mandate and working modality, it was not possible to physically visit all districts across the country to collect damage data. Due to shortage of personnel and time, as well as the failure of contacted agencies to provide information in the prescribed format within the stipulated deadline, a complete damage assessment could not be made. Therefore, the details mentioned in this report regarding damage caused during the demonstrations, as well as the National Planning Commission's report, must both be taken as reference. The detailed breakdown of the said damage has been attached in Annex 4.
Chapter - 5: Statements and Incident Record Documents Received from Stakeholders
Recommendations:
5.1 Statement and Incident Record Documents from Officials in Public Responsibility:
5.1.1 Political Figures
1 (a). Statement of Dr. Deepak Karki, Secretary of the President's Office, on behalf of Honorable President Ram Chandra Paudel:

The statement indicates that had the issues arising in the areas of state administration and management regarding the demonstrations and damage that occurred on Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082 been addressed in a timely and appropriate manner, the damage might perhaps have been less. Information was being received that establishments including Singha Durbar, the Supreme Court, Parliament House, as well as Nepal Police units were being targeted. The Nepal Army had briefed the President on security matters. A letter regarding the then Prime Minister KP Sharma Oli's resignation arrived at approximately 2:45 PM for approval, and after the Prime Minister's resignation was accepted at around 2:50 PM, at approximately 3:00 PM we moved from the President's residence to a secure location provided by the Nepal Army. Subsequently, information arrived that arson and vandalism had occurred at the physical assets, government records, and premises of the President's Office.

Regarding the question of what your view is on the questions raised by the Gen Z generation about the increase in corruption and financial irregularities in the country after Nepal's new constitution and federalism, the statement indicates that Nepal's constitution and the governance arrangements therein provide strong constitutional provisions for Nepal's independence, sovereignty, territorial integrity, national unity, self-reliance, self-respect, democratic value systems, socio-cultural solidarity, proportional participatory arrangements, protection of human rights, and democratic governance  therefore, the country's problems are not caused by the constitution. It may be that the responsible and authorized individuals and institutions failed to timely manage the questions raised about officials in executive roles of the state and to promptly correct the sectoral shortcomings visible in the country.

According to Nepal's constitution, the President serves as the Head of State, promoter of national unity, and guardian and protector of the constitution. Functions performed by the President are governed by Nepal's constitution and federal law. As per constitutional provisions, the President regularly receives information from the Prime Minister regarding current affairs in the country, matters of foreign relations, decisions of the Government of Nepal, and bills presented in the Federal Parliament. Except for informal discussions on specific matters, all functions performed by the President are carried out in accordance with constitutional and legal provisions.

Regarding the events of Bhadra 23, the statement says that continuously receiving information about what was happening, and drawing the attention of responsible government officials to take initiatives for the protection of life and immediate normalization of the situation, was carried out in accordance with constitutional responsibility. The events of Bhadra 24  including casualties, vandalism, looting, and arson  are unfortunate. One can imagine that perhaps less damage would have occurred that day had a political solution been reached in time; however, as the circumstances were highly complex, abnormal, and extraordinary in nature, the difficulties in immediate management by the security forces and other responsible state institutions were clearly evident. As it was necessary to immediately address the extreme and extraordinary situation that had arisen in the country in accordance with the spirit and intent of constitutional provisions, and to normalize the severely strained security situation, efforts were made toward that end. Given the extremely complex and unfortunate circumstances, continuous consultation and efforts were made throughout that day to normalize the security situation.
1. Statement of Former Prime Minister KP Sharma Oli:

After the Tinkune incident in Kathmandu in Chaitra 2082, instructions were given to all security agencies to minimize casualties and prevent infiltration by unwanted elements in the context of such incidents. Since the Home Minister serves as the chairperson of the Central Security Committee, and security sensitivities and related information are with the Home Minister, the then Home Minister was asked to appear before the Commission for answers. Many matters must have come to light through the Home Minister's responses. In fact, the events of Bhadra 23 and 24 were inconceivable and barbaric  the likes of which had never occurred in the country before.

Regarding immediate and rapidly unfolding events during the demonstrations by the Gen Z generation on Bhadra 23, these are not matters to be directly reported to the Prime Minister. The Prime Minister does not issue orders to the police  the Ministry of Home Affairs serves as the relevant line ministry for that. Logically, the Prime Minister is not accountable for this matter. The unruly acts of attacking security personnel themselves, snatching uniforms and guns from security personnel and taking selfies  these were certainly not carried out by the Gen Z members who had declared the movement, but were the acts of infiltrator groups taking advantage of the movement. The Gen Z members themselves have stated that their demands were hijacked, that their intent was not this, and that it was a peaceful movement.

The attempt to set Parliament on fire occurred on the 23rd itself. On that day, groups using youth as a vanguard to achieve their own objectives deliberately created agitation among the youth, leading to a situation where lives were lost. The arson and attacks on all three branches of government were premeditated events. The Parliament House  the seat of representation of the sovereign people  the Singha Durbar  the administrative center of the country  and the Supreme Court  the seat of justice  were all deliberately, selectively targeted, with files examined and data searched before being burned. Videos of those involved have been made public. Have their statements been taken by the Commission?

Is burning industries not burning the country's economy? There were attacks on the social fabric, attacks on the economic order, and actions aimed at destroying our culture and reducing the country to rubble. Can investment be promoted this way? The Prime Minister does not have the authority to order police to open fire during events like those of Bhadra 23. Nor should that be said. Technical questions like these must have been answered by the concerned individuals. Following the events of Bhadra 23, a cabinet meeting was held that evening and decisions were made: to form a judicial commission for impartial investigation of the events, to provide effective treatment to the injured and relief to the families of the deceased, and to lift the ban on social media imposed as per the demands of the Gen Z generation. On Bhadra 24, I submitted my resignation. If that movement occurred because I was an obstacle, then everything should have been fine after my resignation  but it wasn't, was it?

Regarding the regulation of social media  the government made a decision, and social media regulation is necessary! Should one not be accountable? Of course one should! That is precisely why the decision was made. The state officials in positions of responsibility at that time have already given their statements before the Commission on the matter, and I have repeatedly shared my views publicly on this matter.
2. Statement of Former Prime Minister Sher Bahadur Deuba:

On Bhadra 23, 2082, I was at my private residence located at Basukinath Marg, Ward No. 2, Budhanilkantha. That evening, I was also present at Baluwatar and had requested the then Prime Minister to lift the ban on social media, which he did  however, I did not participate in any formal meeting. On the morning of Bhadra 24, at around 10 AM, demonstrators gathered at my private residence and were chanting slogans. Despite security personnel trying to stop them, we were physically assaulted, and some Gen Z youth came to our rescue. We reached a safe location at around 10:00 PM.

The panchanama conducted by Nepal Police in the presence of the Ward Chairperson mentions cash found at my residence  the amount was not large. The social media details must be AI-generated. No report had been filed at any police office in this regard. This is a complete conspiracy, but it is not known who did it. Nepal Police has recorded the panchanama and other details regarding the vandalism, arson, and looting at my residence, and I have no information about any action taken. I have not made any political interference or shown bias, and I do not agree that there was political interference in the police's failure to assess the situation during the events of the 23rd and 24th. Trade union rights are fundamental rights in democracy and should be able to be exercised for practical rights and interests. Student organizations should also be active for broader interests. Non-political institutions or mechanisms in the country must not be politicized. Anyone involved in vandalism, looting, or arson must be held accountable. Policy, legal, and institutional reforms must be made incorporating the sentiments of the youth.
3. Statement of Former Prime Minister Pushpa Kamal Dahal 'Prachanda':

On Bhadra 23, I publicly condemned the crackdown, made a public appeal to declare the fallen demonstrators as martyrs and to restore calm, and called for an all-party meeting the following day. On Bhadra 24, I participated in the all-party meeting at Singha Durbar in the morning, and as the situation became increasingly complex, I remained under the security forces' protection in the afternoon. In my understanding, the backdrop to this movement was the growing public dissatisfaction and anger toward the government's working style. The ban imposed on social media appears to have been the immediate trigger. On Bhadra 23, the youth's anger was expressed in the form of protests, while on the 24th, I have reached the conclusion that the nature of the movement changed and was exploited by wrong elements.

On Bhadra 24, the rented residence where I had been staying in Kathmandu and my house in Chitwan were vandalized, set on fire, and looted. I do not have my own house in Kathmandu. The claim that illegal cash was found at my daughter Ganga Dahal's house is baseless. No such cash was found, nor have we earned income from any illegal source. In my view, the vandalism, arson, and looting at Singha Durbar, Shital Niwas, Parliament House, the Supreme Court, and various government and private buildings on Bhadra 24 were not merely an expression of anger against the government, but a premeditated attempt to strike at the system itself. While my party supported the spirit of the Gen Z movement, it has no involvement in violent activities. If any person affiliated with our party is proven to have been involved in activities contrary to the law, our clear position is that they must face legal consequences. The possibility of both internal and external infiltration is visible in the events of Bhadra 24. The youth themselves had, on Bhadra 23, urged people to return home citing infiltration of the movement. The subsequent chain of events indicates that wrong elements exploited the sentiments of the youth. During my tenure as Prime Minister, I made efforts to prioritize meritocracy in appointment, transfer, and promotion processes. The practices adopted in institutions such as Tribhuvan University have been exemplary. Political interference has been negatively impacting the professionalism and service delivery of the police, civil service, judicial service, and constitutional bodies  though this is not the only cause: the political system, economic structure, and geopolitical positioning are also responsible factors. Various efforts and programs for good governance, social justice, and economic reform were advanced under my leadership. I believe that an objective evaluation of those policies, programs, and budgets will enable understanding of the reasons for the government's fall and the reality of the public anger that arose thereafter. Trade union rights are constitutionally guaranteed rights, but the state machinery must not be used as a political platform. Appointments and promotions in the civil service and other agencies must be based on merit, competence, and experience. Student organizations must play a role in educational reform  not in disrupting education or engaging in indecent activities. To prevent such events from recurring in the future, people-centered programs for economic, social, cultural, and physical development must be implemented. In particular, policies ensuring youth-centered opportunities, employment, and participation must be adopted. I expect the Commission to conduct its evaluation objectively without any preconceptions.
4. Statement of Speaker of the Federal Parliament House of Representatives, Dev Raj Ghimire:

I have been serving as Speaker of the Federal Parliament House of Representatives since Magh 5, 2079. My primary duties, as stipulated by the Federal Parliament Secretariat Act, 2064 and the House of Representatives Rules, 2079, include conducting parliamentary sessions, determining the agenda, authenticating bills, and providing guidance to the Parliament Secretariat.

On Bhadra 23, 2082, I was busy at the Federal Parliament Secretariat, Singha Durbar, with the oath-taking of the chairperson of the Parliamentary Hearing Committee and the authentication of bills. On Bhadra 24, I was at my residence in Baluwatar. The security of the Federal Parliament building is the responsibility of the security forces under the Ministry of Home Affairs  the Speaker has no legal authority in this matter. Although general discussions take place before the commencement of a session, no one had anticipated that the Gen Z movement would pose a risk of this magnitude, and I had received no special information either. Therefore, no Contingency Plan, Evacuation Protocol, or written emergency plan had been prepared for such a situation.

In my understanding, the reasons why some demonstrators managed to enter the Parliament building premises on Bhadra 23 were: the protesters turned out in far greater numbers and in a different form than security personnel had anticipated, security preparations were insufficient, and there was no effective coordination among the security agencies. The structural weaknesses in the Parliament building's walls, gates, and infrastructure also appear to have compounded the security challenges. However, since the responsibility for such structural improvements and security infrastructure falls under the executive and the Ministry of Home Affairs, no special initiative had previously been taken by me in my capacity as Speaker. I received information in the afternoon of Bhadra 23 that gunshots had been fired within the Parliament building premises. Thereafter, I directed the Secretary-General of the Parliament Secretariat to coordinate with the security agencies as necessary. Regarding the vandalism and arson at the Parliament building on Bhadra 24, there are no reports of major casualties  only some minor injuries.

Apart from questions raised regarding the principles and proportionality of the use of force, my assessment is that the security personnel made every possible effort to protect state property, the Parliament building, and the lives and property of those present there, even under dangerous circumstances. The principles of use of force, the necessity and justification of firing, and similar matters are issues that must be evaluated by the relevant experts and agencies based on security policy, law, and the circumstances prevailing at the time  in my view. These matters do not fall within the direct jurisdiction of the Speaker. There is no legal provision enabling the Speaker to directly order the Prime Minister, Home Minister, or security agencies to halt the use of firearms. To prevent such events from recurring in the future, my suggestion is that a system based on a Single Command structure should be established for the security of the Federal Parliament building, with clear protocols based on periodic Threat Analysis and deployment of the required number of security personnel.
5. Statement of Narayan Dahal, Chairperson of the National Assembly of the Federal Parliament:

I have been serving in the capacity of Chairperson of the National Assembly since Falgun 29, 2080. Under the Constitution and the National Assembly Rules, 2075 (with amendments), my responsibilities include conducting assembly sessions, ensuring the sessions are dignified and orderly, and systematically advancing the agenda and procedures within the assembly. The overall security of the Federal Parliament building and secretariat is a responsibility of the Government of Nepal. On Bhadra 23, 2082, in the afternoon, I was in my own office chamber in Building No. 2 of the Federal Parliament Secretariat at Singha Durbar, preparing for a National Assembly session called for 1:15 PM that day. As the situation was not conducive, I issued a notice through the Secretary-General of the Federal Parliament and the Secretary of the National Assembly to convene the session at 3:15 PM, and as circumstances became increasingly complex, I directed that the public be informed that the session was postponed until further notice. On Bhadra 24, I remained at my residence until approximately 1:30 PM, after which I went toward the Police Headquarters at Naxal at around 2:30 PM. Previously, there was no practice of formal prior discussions or directives regarding demonstrations centered around the Federal Parliament building. The details of which security agencies were deployed, under whose command, and in what numbers during the events of Bhadra 23 and 24 fall within the government's area of responsibility, so I did not have detailed information on that. Since the Parliament building is a sensitive area declared a prohibited zone by the Government of Nepal, such a situation had not even been imagined. Overall security management falls within the policy and implementation responsibilities of the government. I was under the belief that coordination was ongoing, as the Parliament Security Force is a unit under the Ministry of Home Affairs. My understanding is that the security personnel must have followed directives received through their chain of command. Learning from the events of the 23rd and 24th, I have felt the need for a separate legal arrangement for the security of the Federal Parliament. On Bhadra 23, I was not at the Parliament building in Baneswhar but rather in my own office chamber at Singha Durbar. On Bhadra 24, I received information from the security agencies that demonstrators had entered the Parliament building and engaged in vandalism and arson. After adverse circumstances developed on Bhadra 24, I remained within the security cordon along with family members. Although no vandalism occurred at my official or private residence in Kathmandu, my private residence in Chitwan was set on fire. Maintaining peace and public order falls within the jurisdiction of the executive. Legal and structural reforms are necessary to prevent such events from recurring in the future.
6. Statement of Then Home Minister Ramesh Lekhak:

On Bhadra 23 and 24, regarding the difficult security situation that emerged in various parts of the country  particularly the Kathmandu Valley  I, in my capacity as Home Minister, bore the constitutional and legal responsibility to maintain overall peace and security. Before the events began, I had been receiving information through security agencies about growing dissatisfaction and anger on social media and through other channels, and on that basis, the Ministry of Home Affairs was continuously coordinating with Nepal Police, the Armed Police Force, and the National Investigation Department to monitor the situation. I clarify that I did not issue direct commands in the field, but rather discharged my responsibility of providing policy directives, coordination, and necessary facilitation at the political level. The Government of Nepal's Work Division Rules assign the Ministry of Home Affairs the tasks of policy, standards, law, plan implementation, and regulation related to internal security and law and order, and the execution thereof has been done through agencies under the ministry in accordance with relevant laws. The reporting and directives between the Home Minister, the ministry's administrative body, and the police administration also operated as per the relevant laws, with direct contact occasionally occurring as necessary. Since the proposal to take a decision regarding social media at the Council of Ministers did not come from the Ministry of Home Affairs, there was no separate process of seeking the security agencies' opinions on the matter. On the evening of Bhadra 22, a meeting of the National Security Council was held at Baluwatar, where information was provided by all security agencies  including the National Investigation Department and Nepal Police  that Gen Z youth would demonstrate peacefully on Bhadra 23, and no concrete intelligence of potential violence had been received. In my capacity as Home Minister, in the context of the Bhadra 23 demonstrations, I had issued directives to the Home Secretary and the heads of security agencies to ensure no casualties occurred during the use of force, to ensure no one died, to make appropriate coordinated peace and security arrangements, and to remain alert to the risk that the movement could be leaderless since it was not led by a political group, and to be vigilant about potential infiltration.

On Bhadra 23, as clashes between demonstrators and police were occurring inside and outside the Parliament building premises, my position was that the Federal Parliament building had to be secured at all costs  but I had not issued any order for the use of lethal force, i.e., to open fire. The use of force is a matter to be decided by deployed officers based on ground-level conditions, and there is no practice of the Home Minister issuing direct orders on such matters. I have made clear in my statement that there is no legal provision or practical precedent for the Inspector General of Police to seek written or oral authorization from the Home Minister for the use of lethal force, and therefore this was not done. Upon receiving information that the demonstration was intensifying and turning violent toward Baneswhar, I informed the Honorable Prime Minister, the Honorable Defense Minister, and the Chief of Army Staff. Upon receiving news of casualties from gunfire, I immediately consulted with Ministry of Home Affairs officials and security agencies to arrange for citizen safety and to stop casualties, and directed administration to facilitate treatment for the injured. Due to the tragic and unimaginable events of Bhadra 23, I was deeply grieved and distressed, and out of the immense anguish I felt, I immediately took moral responsibility and resigned from the position of Home Minister. I have publicly stated that the demands raised by the Gen Z generation on Bhadra 23 were fundamentally correct  but I firmly believe that the tragic events that occurred after infiltration of that noble spirit, and the attacks on the state's historic and invaluable structures, cannot be limited to being described merely as the result of emotions. The statements by Gen Z leaders saying 'our movement has been successful, it is being hijacked and turned violent, friends please return home' confirmed for me the fact that there had been infiltration of the demonstrations. I am firm in my position that the events of Bhadra 24 must be viewed separately from the demands of the youth, and that anyone involved in criminal activities must face legal consequences. Demonstrators entered my private homes in Naikap, Kathmandu, and Mahendranagar, Kanchanpur, and vandalized, burned, and looted them. The new Police Bill concerning Nepal Police and the Armed Police Force had been registered in Parliament and was under discussion in the State Affairs Committee. I have acknowledged with self-criticism aspects such as the weaknesses visible in the state's security system, the lack of intelligence collection and analysis, the weakness of the state's dialogue with the younger generation, and insufficient preparedness. I have pointed out the need to clarify legal reforms, capacity enhancement of security agencies, human rights-friendly crowd management, inter-agency coordination, use of technology, and political-administrative accountability for long-term peace and security assurance.
7. Statement of Then Communications Minister Prithivi Subba Gurung:

While serving as Minister of Communication and Information Technology, I considered the matter of social media to not merely be a technical question for the government, but a serious matter connected to legal, policy, and public interest concerns. The Government of Nepal had repeatedly published public notices requesting social media platforms to register with the ministry, designate contact points and contact persons within Nepal, and come under the government's regulatory framework. In the meantime, the Honorable Supreme Court had already issued a mandamus to regulate social media, and the 'Directive on Regulating the Use of Social Media, 2080' had also made registration and contact point designation mandatory. Therefore, the then government had proceeded with the social media regulation process to implement these legal and policy provisions, and accordingly, necessary steps were taken as per the Cabinet decision of 2082/05/09.

On Bhadra 23, I was in my ministerial office chamber at Singha Durbar and later participated in a meeting with the Honorable Prime Minister. The social media regulation bill had already been prepared before I became minister; it was sent to Parliament in my tenure after necessary revisions. Through that bill, provisions were sought to bring social media platforms under a licensing system, ensure registration, contact points, grievance hearings, content regulation, user protection, and legal accountability. Its primary objective was to regulate social media to be organized and accountable while controlling digital crime, personal defamation, misinformation, and misleading information. In my capacity as minister, I consulted with the ministry's secretary, joint secretary, expert leadership of the regulatory body, and security mechanisms within my contacts, and ultimately the Cabinet's decision was implemented. The decision to restrict social media was not taken out of personal impulse but was taken as a duty to comply with court orders, directive provisions, and Cabinet decisions. Platforms like Reddit and Discord were not separately identified and prioritized as high-risk. After the events of Bhadra 23, I participated in continuous discussions and consultations with the Home Minister and others while remaining at the Prime Minister's Office. After analyzing the chain of events, the process to reopen social media was initiated that very night as per the decision, and this involved the collective decision of the Prime Minister, Chief Secretary, and others.
8. Statement of Rabi Lamichhane:

Until Bhadra 23, 2082, conditions in the prison were normal. However, on Bhadra 24, at around 12:30 PM, prison wardens summoned me to the office, and representatives of the security agencies informed me that an abnormal situation had developed outside the prison, that a large crowd had gathered, and that it had become necessary to hand me over to family and party and send me outside. I refused to leave the prison without a court order. Nevertheless, after the prison administration repeatedly requested that I personally be able to calm the crowd, I agreed to address the crowd while remaining within the prison premises and asked for a microphone to be arranged. As the police were in a panicked state, the crowd pushed me and took me outside. Thereafter, I climbed to the balcony of a restaurant near the prison and addressed the crowd, appealing for peace, restraint, and protection of public property. The prison administration had apparently been preparing to give me a formal release letter, but before the letter was ready, the crowd took me out of the prison. I later received information that a letter had come from the prison, but I had no participation in signing it or in the writing process, so the prison administration itself must be accountable for that letter. In reality, I was handed over to the crowd under the direction of the prison administration itself and was forcibly sent outside even though I did not want to leave.

Clarifying that neither I nor my party had any involvement in the events of Bhadra 24, I and the Rastriya Swatantra Party have stated that we played no direct or indirect role in any violent activities, and a commitment has been expressed that if anyone affiliated with us is found to have been involved, they will face strict legal consequences. After leaving the prison, with the help of well-wishers, I reached a safe location via artist Ramkrishna Dhakal's vehicle, and immediately informed the chiefs of Nepal Police, the Armed Police Force, and the Nepal Army about my whereabouts. That same evening, I also informed the public through a video message that 'I am safe and will appear in court again.' On Bhadra 28, after receiving assurances of security, I returned to Nakkhu Prison. Learning from these events, I suggest adopting an open prison policy for prison reform, strengthening mental health and legal counseling systems for prisoners, and establishing a prisoner review mechanism.
